---
title: 'Git Spring 2026'
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: solarized
    resources: ["images"]
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: true
  message: true
---

## Pair and Share

Look at your partner's Quarto - Any suggestions?

## File Organization

Recall from ESM-206

## Suggested Organization

-   R Project for this course

-   Folders for R scripts for functions (TBD)

-   Sub folders for each Week, and within them Quarto, Data, Images

Create this as a new project 

Add you Assignment 1, and the data file you used for Assignment 1 to the Week1 Folder

## Reproducibility 

-   Why reproducibility and what does it mean?

-   What is a workflow?

    -   not usually linear
    -   often involves iterative steps
    -   often involves multiple people

## Reproducibility and Git/Github

 Github is

## Git Concept

Alison Figure

## Git Concept More
[Local Git]{style="color:green"}

-   keep track of changes to your code on your computer
-   when necessary, go back to early versions

[GitHub]{style="color:green"}

-   access your code from different places
-   collaborate with others
    -   others who will use your code
    -   coding together

![git figure with connections]("week2/gitandgithub.jpg")

##  Example 

[GitHub Repository that I'll use in this  course](https://github.com/naomitague/ESM_262_Examples)

## The Basics 

[ Commit]{style="color:green"}

-   saves the current version locally each commit has a label so you can backtrack if needed
-   you can look at what changes between commits
-   gives you an opportunity to comment on what changes

[ Push/Pull]{style="color:green"}

-   **push** integrates any local commits/changes to central GitHub\
-   **pull** integrates any commits/changes from central GitHb to local

[ History]{style="color:green"}

-   shows your sequence of commit messages

[ Diff]{style="color:green"}

-   shows what changed in a file since last commit

[ Revert]{style="color:green"}

-   go back to last commit GitHub's [Git Guides](https://github.com/git-guides) are a really wonderful resource to refer to!

## [Lets try]{style="color:orange"}

Steps

1.  In *GitHub*, create a new repository; you will use this for an assignment, so name it appropriately.

    -   Make it a public repo.

    -   Get the link for cloning.

2.  In *Rstudio* open your class project;

    -   put it in a new directory

    -   make sure you enable *git* in project set up (if you miss this go to Project Options)

    -   set your link to *GitHub* repository

3.  Add a new file

    -   create a subdirectory called *R*

    -   move/copy your function from the last assignment to this subdirectory

4.  Tell Git (local) about your new file

    -   go to the commit section

    -   you new file will have a ?, check the box it will turn to A for add

    -   add a commit message; somethink like "added a function to do X to the project"

    -   click commit

5.  Now change something in your R code; you could just add a comment ,then go to the Git tab

    -   add your change and save the file

    -   see how this shows up in git diff

6.  Once you like what you changed, commit it

    -   your file should have an *M* for modified, check this to "stage" your changes for commit

    -   add a message briefly describing what you changed

    -   commit

7.  Send changes to *github* by pushing

8.  Go to *github* and check to see if your file and recent changes are there

# [Add another file]{style="color:orange"}

1.  Create a new file - maybe a Quatro file that will do something with your R function - add 1-2 lines

2.  Save it into the appropriate directory ( using organized directory structures is good programming practice )

3.  Add it to Git local

    -   go to Git and then commit

    -   find your file, should have a "?", check so that it becomes an "A"

    -   add a commit message

    -   commit

4.  Continue to edit your file but this time add something you don't want

    -   make a change and save

    -   go to your file and look at the changes

    -   click revert to get rid of the changes that you don't want

    -   look at file to make sure unwanted edit is not there

We will talk about how to get something back *after* you've committed it next class

## A few things to note

* if you are starting an R project from scratch, you can check the option set up a git repository on creation
